Given that the key difference between any funds is reflected in their Total Return indicators, let's take a look at the difference between them. We will analyze a period of almost two years (from March 6, 2024, the launch date of QDTE), for which QDTE's Total Return is 9.60% higher (40.10%). That said, a 30.50% return for FEPI is still pretty good. Also, for the last year, there's been a really small difference between the funds (QDTE is up 0.57%).

Risks to a "Buy" for FEPI

Given that I have chosen FEPI over QDTE and recommend "Buy" for the former, there is a need to assess the potential risks associated with its maximum concentration of capital in a small number of stocks (15 holdings). Due to this, there is limited diversification in this fund, so if even one company fails, performance (reflected by NAV), can drop significantly. As an example, a 10% drop in one of the stocks in the portfolio would impact NAV potentially in the mid-single digits.

While FEPI is capable of more effectively realizing the growth potential of stocks, it still has a limited maximum return. As a result, even a 5-10% rally in a single day does not allow the fund to capture all possible profits. Furthermore, during a severe bear market, there is no real protection for FEPI against a significant drop in its NAV. While the premium received, paid out as dividends to shareholders, serves as a partial buffer and is limited to the premium income, this is not a real mechanism for protecting against falling prices. As a result, if an AI crisis develops in the market, then the fund's performance will be correspondingly negative.

Conclusion

Therefore, comparative analysis between FEPI and QDTE reveals fundamental differences between them. I consider QDTE to be a more aggressive instrument, ideally suited for generating stable weekly income, yet it may yield adverse results in the long term due to NAV erosion. My recommendation for QDTE is therefore to "Hold," but I recommend "Buy" for FEPI. One thing I like is that this fund is not only betting on Mag 7, as well as semiconductor companies. This fund is suitable for investors like me who believe in the continuation of the AI supercycle in 2026. In addition, it offers a high dividend yield of around 25%, ideal for those who are dividend-oriented and seek to reinvest their payments back into the assets they have purchased.

data.title: Bio-Rad Laboratories Still Hampered By Underwhelming Core Growth
data.description:
Bio-Rad's (BIO) (BIO.B) management has been taking steps to improve its long-term core growth prospects, but it takes time for those steps to translate into real results. Meanwhile, the company has to deal with challenging end-market conditions, including considerable funding uncertainties for academic labs and biotechs, as well as global tariffs.

I wasn’t that bullish on Bio-Rad a year ago, and the shares have seen meaningful volatility since then. Shares sold off sharply in the first half of 2025 on weak results and guidance before rallying around 60%. Net-net, the shares are still down about 15% since my last update, worse than peers/comps like Agilent (A), Bio-Techne (TECH), Qiagen (QGEN), and Thermo Fisher (TMO).

Bio-Rad’s valuation continues to be heavily influenced by its roughly one-third stake in Germany’s Sartorius (SARTF). I could argue for 20% or more upside based on improving end-market conditions, particularly Sartorius’s bioprocessing business, but I still don’t fundamentally like Bio-Rad’s business all that much, and I think investors who want exposure to Sartorius would be better served owning Sartorius. That said, expectations are low, and even relatively modest progress in revenue growth and/or margins could drive re-rating.

Waiting For The End Of A Volatile Year With Volatile Guidance

Bio-Rad investors have had a bit of a rollercoaster ride regarding company guidance this year. While management started off with 2.5% core revenue growth guidance for 2025 (at the midpoint), that number moved to almost zero before rebounding a bit, with adjusted operating margin guidance likewise moving from 13%-13.5% initially to 10%-12% at the worst and then to 12.5%-13.5% as the tariff situation between the U.S. and China evolved.

This leaves a wide spread of potential outcomes for Q4, including potentially mid-single-digit growth at the high end, with the current sell-side average estimate at 4%. Underlying Life Sciences revenue should be up low single digits given recent trends and guidance from other life science tools companies, with some possible upside from end-of-year budget spending on tools like the company’s droplet digital PCR (or ddPCR) instruments. On the other hand, while process chromatography has rebounded this year and should be up double digits again in Q4 (against a roughly 50% year-over-year drop), this has been a tough line item to model.

Diagnostics is likely to see low growth in the quarter. Bio-Rad has been seeing steady, though not particularly spectacular, demand for quality control and immunology products, offset by weaker diabetes testing in China. That headwind should ease in Q4 as the company annualizes that impact, letting the low single-digit underlying growth come through.

Weak guides for margins have set the stage for multiple quarters of EBITDA and operating income beats, but I expect this to be a little better dialed in for the fourth quarter, and while I see some upside, I don’t expect a blowout (I’m looking for around $125M in adjusted EBITDA).

A Better Year Ahead, But Likely Still Pressured

There have been a few positive developments in Bio-Rad’s end markets, but the overall environment is still challenging, and that doesn’t bode well for a return to the 3% to 5% long-term growth range that management has previously targeted.

On the positive side, a federal appeals court recently upheld a ruling that blocks the NIH from capping reimbursement for indirect overhead costs at 15% of grant awards, and these indirect reimbursements are an important source of funding for lab equipment. It remains to be seen what, if anything, the administration does in response. Even so, there’s another budget deadline on the way, and it seems unlikely that there’s much appetite for a meaningful (if any) increase in NIH funding.

While the NIH is a limited part of Bio-Rad’s business (around 4% by management’s estimation) and overall U.S. academic research is less than 10% of revenue, there’s still a trickle-down effect from weaker NIH funding. Likewise, the biopharma funding environment has only recently started to improve from weak levels, and multiple life science tools companies have pointed to weaker spending from this sector as a driver of weaker sales.

More positively, a substantial amount of Bio-Rad’s revenue comes from transfusion, reference, and hospital labs where the spending environment is more stable. It remains to be seen what the elimination of ACA subsidies will do to healthcare utilization (including lab work), but this tends to be a more stable business, and I expect that to remain the case.

Still Waiting For ddPCR To Accelerate

One of the challenges with Bio-Rad from a stock-picking perspective is the lack of meaningful growth drivers. While the company has high-level exposure to growth segments like proteomics, cell biology, and bioproduction, it doesn’t tend to be with high-profile, high-growth instruments or consumables.

The one exception is the company’s droplet digital PCR business. The use case for ddPCR is that it produces more sensitive, more accurate, and more reproducible results faster and more cost-effectively than alternatives like next-gen sequencing or qPCR.

Management recently revised its addressable market here from over $10 billion to around $5 billion, acknowledging that the diagnostics opportunity is still in its very early stages (with significant long-term opportunities in liquid biopsy and residual disease detection). Even with management refocusing on the nearer-term opportunities in life sciences (targeted sequencing and relative quantitation for drug discovery, translational research, and bioprocessing), the market is still likely only around 10% penetrated today, with Bio-Rad enjoying a good initial lead ahead of Qiagen and Thermo Fisher.

Management has also been spending to build up its capabilities here. Bio-Rad spent over $218 million upfront to acquire Stilla Technologies and its lower-ASP ddPCR system. Coupled with its QX Continuum platform, Bio-Rad now has a fuller array of solutions for this market, and I do see it as a double-digit growth opportunity extending over several years—particularly if/when it becomes a core enabling technology for liquid biopsies.

The Outlook

Guidance for better than 2% revenue growth in FY’26 would likely be considered a win by the Street, let alone a return to the more normalized 3%-plus growth rate. The latter could be a big ask in an environment of flat NIH funding and still-lackluster biopharma funding, but a stronger year for process chromatography and strength in ddPCR demand/uptake could drive it.

At this point I’m only looking for around 3% core revenue growth over the next three years, as whatever potential recovery may be present in life sciences is offset by a lack of proprietary core drivers outside of ddPCR. Management has been saying more of the right things lately with respect to ROI-driven R&D allocation decisions, but it takes time for R&D projects to translate into revenue growth, and Bio-Rad has never been particularly good at it—again, this is a company that has grown revenue at an annualized rate of less than 5% over the last 20-plus years.

Bio-Rad’s valuation cannot be separated from the value of its stake in Sartorius, including 38% of ordinary shares and 28% of preferred shares. The value and prospects of Sartorius would be a separate article in its own right, so for now I’ll substitute in the sell-side’s price targets for the underlying shares and note that Sartorius’s bioprocessing business does appear to be turning up again (and the long-term case for healthy growth here remains sound). Net of expected taxes, I value Bio-Rad’s stake at around $171/share.

Between discounted cash flow and multiples-based approaches (EV/EBITDA, EV/revenue, et al.), I believe Bio-Rad is worth around $338 to $390 today, with upside if or when management can drive better sustained revenue growth. Low-to-mid single-digit revenue growth isn’t worth much in life sciences, supporting around 11x EBITDA or 1.5x revenue, but a path to high single-digit growth would at least put a 2x EV/revenue multiple in play (and a $400-plus fair value after including the Sartorius stake).

The Bottom Line

Bio-Rad likely is undervalued today, but I still don’t really like the business. I can appreciate the argument that this is a way to leverage a stake in Sartorius with operational execution upside at Bio-Rad, but I just don’t have that confidence in management at this point, and the overall operating environment is still challenging. It’s not well-covered, so upside could take the Street by surprise and drive some rerating upside, but I need more core growth to get really bullish about this name.

data.title: Vornado Realty Trust: Premier NYC Properties, Shares Trade At A Discount
data.description:
Vornado Realty Trust (VNO) owns and operates some of the most premier real estate assets in the New York City market. Its holdings are located primarily in Midtown Manhattan and along the busiest walking corridors of the city. Some of its nationally recognized properties include the global headquarters of Bloomberg, LP and over 10M square feet of property in the Penn District.

I’ve covered VNO previously and have maintained a bullish stance throughout my coverage. My stance has been concentrated about my persistent bullishness on the overall office market following the decimation seen during the COVID-19 pandemic.

While the recovery has been slow, I believe the NYC market has recovered remarkably well. VNO does trade at a premium to its peer NYC operators, but I still view shares as attractive at current trading levels. For investors seeking positioning, I believe VNO is one name that presents upside potential of at least 15%.

VNO Stock Key Metrics

Shares in VNO currently command a forward multiple of funds from operations (“FFO”) of nearly 14x. This compares to about 8x from fellow NYC-based peers Empire State Realty (ESRT) and SL Green (SLG). Sure this is a premium, but I believe the premium is warranted given VNO’s first class property portfolio in the top tier Manhattan market.

Granted, the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quant ratings aren’t in agreement with my assessment, with the metrics grading poorly on valuation. VNO has also graded poorly on its growth prospects and momentum, likely due to its underperformance over the past year in relation to the broader sector.

On the flip side, while most Wall Street analysts are neutral on the stock, VNO was recently upgraded following its Q3 earnings release. The average price target on the stock now stands just shy of $40/share, which would represent about 17% upside from current trading levels. In my view, VNO’s recent operating performance would justify any move higher in the near-medium term.

VNO Operating Performance

Vornado turned in a strong Q3 when it released results back in early November. Consistent with prior quarters, the story continues to center on leasing momentum and improving property-level fundamentals.

Across the portfolio, demand remained concentrated in top-tier Manhattan assets, where VNO operates almost exclusively. During the quarter, the company executed 21 NY office leases totaling 594K square feet (“SQF”) at an impressive average starting rent of $103/PSF.

Even more notable, mark-to-market spreads came in at +15.7% on a GAAP basis and +10.4% on a cash basis, with average lease terms extending beyond 12 years. The length of term is especially notable, in my view, and it represents a clear sign of tenant confidence.

The PENN District remains one of VNO’s best held assets. At PENN 2, VNO leased 325K SQF in Q3 at average rents of $112/PSF, pushing occupancy to 78% and keeping the property on track to exceed year-end guidance.

It’s worth noting that within the asset, leasing since project inception now exceeds 1.3M SQF, and the management team expects incremental cash yields to move higher. PENN 1 also continued to outperform underwriting, with steady leasing at strong rents following redevelopment.

Portfolio-wide, NY office occupancy rose to 88.4%, up from 86.7% last quarter, driven primarily by PENN 2 activity. Importantly, the leasing pipeline remains healthy, with over 1.1M SQF currently in negotiation.

All said and done, Q3 showed that VNO is benefiting from a tightening Class A office market, where high-quality space is becoming increasingly scarce.

What Is The Outlook For VNO Stock?

Looking ahead, I view VNO’s outlook as positive. This is even as headline risks, such as the recent outcome of the mayoral election, continue to weigh on sentiment.

In its Q3 commentary, CEO Steven Roth made it clear on his call that on-the-ground fundamentals in NYC remain strong. In his commentary, he noted that leasing demand for high-quality office space is not only holding up, but accelerating, with vacancy in prime Midtown assets tightening and rents pushing higher.

With regards to leasing, VNO expects 2025 Manhattan office leasing volume to be its strongest in over a decade, supported by a deep pipeline and continued absorption at PENN 1 and PENN 2. Furthermore, occupancy is projected to move into the low-90% range over the next year, setting the stage for meaningful earnings growth as free rent burns off.

The improving setup was recently flagged by the sell side. Analysts at J.P. Morgan (JPM), for example, recently upgraded VNO from ‘Underweight’ to ‘Neutral’ following the stock’s pullback, citing improving fundamentals and continued leasing strength despite political and macro concerns.

Importantly, JPM noted that fears around a potentially business-unfriendly NYC administration have not translated into tenant hesitation, a view echoed by Vornado’s own commentary.

Near term, management expects FFO to remain relatively stable through 2026 as redevelopment activity temporarily takes income offline, and I believe this is a reasonable expectation.

Is VNO Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I continue to view VNO as a 'Buy', and I believe this view is supported by VNO’s most recently released results in Q3 and its forward outlook ahead.

Leasing momentum remains a key catalyst, in my view. VNO is executing at a level that few office REITs can currently match. Strong mark-to-market spreads, long lease terms, and rising occupancy, particularly across the PENN District, also signals that demand for top-tier Manhattan office space is not only intact but strengthening.

With over 1.1M SQF in active negotiations and expectations for full year 2025 leasing to be the strongest in more than a decade, operating fundamentals appear to be moving solidly in the right direction.

The improving outlook was also recognized in JPM’s recent upgrade, alongside a roughly $40/share price target. Their perspective in their note reflected growing confidence that VNO’s leasing strength can offset lingering macro and political concerns. In my view, that target is reasonable and achievable if VNO continues to deliver positive leasing and operating results over the coming quarters.

At current trading levels, a move toward $40 would imply upside of at least 15%. While VNO currently trades at a premium to its NYC peers, I believe the premium is justified given the quality of its assets, its balance sheet progress, and its positioning in a tightening Class A office market. For investors seeking exposure to a recovering office sector, I believe VNO stands out as an attractive opportunity.

data.title: Old Republic: Q4 Underwriting Results Add To Margin Fears
data.description:
Shares of Old Republic International (ORI) plunged over 10% on Thursday as investors digested disappointing earnings, and this drop erased most of the gains shares had generated over the past year, leaving them up just 6%. I last covered shares in October, rating the stock a “hold” given valuation, and shares are now flat, including a special dividend, after today’s drop, which is consistent with the rating. With updated financials and the drop in shares, now is a good time to revisit ORI, especially as shares have reached the $38 level I previously cited as being a good entry point. I would not buy yet given recent developments.

Weak Underwriting Drives a Q4 Miss

In the company’s fourth quarter, Old Republic earned $0.74, which missed expectations by $0.14 as revenue grew 9% to $2.4 billion. This came even as net premiums rose 9.8% to $2.1 billion. Additionally, thanks to higher interest rates on its fixed reinvestment, net investment income was $184 million, up 8% from last year. However, the company’s combined ratio was 96%, which was 3.3% worse than a year ago because Old Republic was forced to add provisions to its long-haul trucking reserves as it is seeing concerning loss trends. As you can see below, Old Republic is primarily a specialty insurer focused on commercial property & casualty policies, though it also has a small title insurance business.

Drilling into segment results, its specialty business is decentralized across several product lines and brands. In general, though, its philosophy is constant across segments. ORI tries to take a “coinsurance” approach with its customers, who are largely national firms in the auto, trucking, construction, and oil & gas sectors. Commercial auto (primarily trucking) is the largest portion of the business, at 42% of net premiums. This coinsurance approach means its policies are either with a captive insurer of its client or by making its client carry a very high deductible. In this way, if claims run higher than expected, both the client and ORI face losses, and if claims are low, both benefit. This aligns interests between ORI and its clients. As a consequence of this structure, its results are typically more stable over time, though its combined ratio tends to run higher (since it is making clients retain more risk).

Q4 Likely Overstated Weakness, but Risks Linger Into 2026

That approach is why the magnitude of the underwriting miss was so surprising. Specialty insurance reported a combined ratio of 97.3%, up from 91.8% last year. The company raised its 2025 loss assumption for commercial auto by 3%, and since this all occurred in Q4, that increased the Q4 loss assumption by ~12%. At 42% of the business, this was a ~5% impact, accounting for nearly all the increase in the combined ratio. In a sense, based on the data it now has, ORI should have reported weaker underwriting margins in Q1-Q3 than it did, and as a result, it had to catch up in Q4.

Insurance policies are reserved based on current actuarial assumptions and then constantly refined as insurers receive more data, so the nature of this action is not unusual in any way, but the magnitude of the change is concerning. However, I would emphasize that because of this, ORI’s underwriting performance in Q4 looked worse than it was (to offset Q1-Q3 being better and ensure an accurate full-year picture). Underwriting variances do occur, and fortunately, these policies tend to be short-tail, so the impact should only be for ~1 year. Nonetheless, for the full year, its combined ratio was 93.2%, which was the worst performance since pre-COVID.

Whenever there is a large revision like this, there is always a concern that there are potentially other underwriting problems waiting to surface, and that is a reason I believe shares have responded so negatively. This is especially the case because there is already fear that industrywide margins have peaked. As you can see below, insurance premium inflation has slowed markedly as the industry recouped margins post-COVID, and now capital is returning, increasing competitiveness. ORI pointing to weaker underwriting margins against this backdrop is setting off alarm bells. While again, I would emphasize that Q4’s combined ratio was overly impacted, I expect the 2026 combined ratio to be flat or a bit worse than the full year 2025 ratio of 93.2%.

Title Revenue Shows Some Momentum

Elsewhere, title revenue was up 12% to $789 million. From an underwriting perspective, its title business was steady with its combined ratio improving 40 bps to 94.0%. It is the third-largest player in the sector with 14% market share, though it focuses a bit more than peers on the commercial market (about 26% of its business for the full year and 29% in Q4). Given well-documented title records in the US, losses are typically small and stable over time. This business does better when there are more real estate transactions, as you typically need to buy title insurance whenever getting a mortgage (which includes refinancing).

As you can see below, volumes are down a third since the post-COVID boom when the housing market surged and low rates led to more refinancing. Assuming mortgage rates average 6-6.25% in 2026, I expect modest growth in this segment going forward. In particular, after years of difficulty, capital market access for commercial properties is improving, especially as a worst-case scenario for the office segment appears unlikely to occur. I expect commercial activity to outpace residential growth in 2026, which is positive for Old Republic, given its business mix, and I expect ~$3.1 billion of title revenue this year.

ORI has a conservative $16.7 billion investment portfolio with no alternative assets. It is 85% invested in fixed income, and 99% of the portfolio is investment grade with an A+ average rating. The portfolio has a 4.3% book yield, and ORI should continue to benefit from the maturity of lower-yielding securities in 2026. I expect the portfolio yield to gravitate towards 4.5%, which supports continued net interest income growth, though Q1 will likely be softer due to the payment of its special dividend. ORI also has a 15% allocation to public equities, primarily focused on dividend stocks.

Capital Returns May Shift Towards Buybacks

Looking at capital returns, shares now offer a 3% ordinary dividend yield. Additionally, given its excess capital position, ORI announced a $2.50 special dividend that was paid earlier this month. During the quarter, ORI repurchased $56 million of stock, and it has $856 million of remaining authorization, which will likely last several years. As you can see, ORI has steadily grown its ordinary dividend, which I expect to continue, whereas special dividends and buybacks are lumpier. Given the decline in shares, I would expect ORI to refocus on buybacks in 2026 and would not expect a similarly sized special dividend this year. I expect about $3 of capital returns in 2026 across its ordinary dividend, buybacks, and any special dividends.

Too Soon to Buy the Dip Given Underwriting Overhang

Back in October, I was targeting $3.45-$3.65 in 2026 earnings. Now, the payment of its special dividend is likely a ~$0.08 headwind to net interest income, and I think we should assume a higher combined ratio on its trucking exposure, at least through H1. As such, I am now targeting $3.25-$3.55 in 2026 EPS, or about a 14% return on equity, as book value ended the quarter at $24. This leaves shares trading right around 11x earnings and with an ~8% capital return yield. This is not expensive, and buybacks can help provide support for shares.

At the same time, investors can buy shares in Travelers (TRV) at a lower multiple and without the recent underwriting missteps. I expect ORI to be in the “penalty box” for several months as investors wait to see if there are further negative underwriting developments, especially given broader pricing pressure. This caps upside in my view. While shares are near my prior price target to buy, that was before ORI paid out a $2.50 special dividend, reducing its book value, and before the added underwriting concern. As such, I would want shares to be closer to $36 before buying. With capital returns and Q4 overstating weakness, I do not view ORI as a “sell,” but it is too soon to actively buy the dip. I remain a hold and recommend investors look elsewhere, like TRV, for better opportunities.

data.title: Rethinking Long/Short Equity: Building On A Foundation Of Stocks, Not Cash
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

When investors hear "long/short equity," they often picture a market-neutral1 hedge fund strategy, one that seeks to profit from the difference between a group of long positions and a group of short positions. The typical benchmark is something like short-term Treasuries, and the goal is to deliver positive absolute returns regardless of what the market does.

That framing has its merits, but it also leaves something important on the table: the power of long-term equity ownership. If we start with the insight that equities have historically been the best long-term inflation hedge and wealth generator, the central thesis of Professor Jeremy Siegel's Stocks for the Long Run,2 then there's a compelling opportunity to rethink how long/short strategies are designed. Instead of beginning with short-term investments, what if we begin with the market itself?

Introducing the WisdomTree Efficient Long/Short U.S. Equity Fund

Today, WisdomTree is bringing this modern approach to market with the launch of the WisdomTree Efficient Long/Short U.S. Equity Fund (WTLS).

The Fund applies the long/short framework described below, building on a core allocation to the S&P 500 while overlaying a dynamic, AI-driven long/short equity strategy designed to pursue market-neutral alpha. WTLS seeks total return by investing roughly 90% of its assets in U.S. large-cap equities and layering on approximately 90% notional exposure to a statistically driven long/short portfolio, resulting in a capital-efficient "portable alpha" structure.

A New Baseline: The U.S. Equity Market as the Core

In this baseline construction, the foundation isn't a Treasury bill; it's the S&P 500 Index. This represents the return of the broad U.S. equity market: the growth of American business, the innovation of its companies and the compounding that has rewarded patient investors for more than a century.

This base exposure is what most investors want over time: the opportunity to participate in the broad U.S. equity market's rise. But on top of that foundation, we can layer a secondary return source, one that differentiates between stronger and weaker companies within that U.S. equity universe.

The result isn't a replacement for equity exposure. It's a potential enhancement.

Layering Relative Strength: Long the Leaders, Short the Laggards

The core idea remains familiar: Identify a group of stocks that exhibit strong relative characteristics—whether momentum, earnings revisions or other indicators of improving fundamentals—and go long those names. At the same time, identify another group showing deteriorating fundamentals or negative relative strength and take short positions there.

What's different here is the sophistication of the signal-generation process. Instead of relying on a handful of metrics, the system digests more than 150 equity features spanning valuation, quality, risk, size, profitability, liquidity and investment characteristics. Two complementary models, a linear instrumented PCA framework3 and a nonlinear autoencoder,4 extract latent risk factors from this broad feature set and forecast relative performance. These model outputs are then combined in a mean-variance (max-Sharpe Ratio) tangent optimization before being mapped back to individual securities.

The result is a long–short structure that typically holds roughly 300 longs and 300 shorts drawn from the top 2,000 U.S. stocks above a liquidity and price threshold.5 Gross exposure remains high (about 100% long/~88% short), while the net exposure varies only modestly. Monthly rebalancing keeps the portfolio aligned with the evolving factor landscape and respects practical constraints, such as short-borrow fees.6

Crucially, the return engine still comes from the spread between strong and weak companies: the historical tendency for winners to keep winning and laggards to continue underperforming. But unlike a traditional standalone long/short equity fund, this is designed as a layered exposure on top of a broad U.S. equity allocation, isolating a source of potential alpha while neutralizing the risk of missing out on broad equity market returns.

From Market-Neutral to Market-Anchored

Traditional long/short portfolios are market-neutral by design, seeking alpha in isolation from beta. This new approach is market-anchored: it embraces beta as the foundation of wealth creation and uses relative-performance insights to generate potential alpha on top.

That distinction has profound implications. Instead of comparing performance to short-term Treasuries, this strategy measures success relative to the equity market itself. It asks not, "Can we make money in any environment?" but rather, "Can we make more of the market's return by emphasizing the right stocks and avoiding the wrong ones?"

Potential Benefits: Efficiency, Diversification and Compounding

Because the overlay can be implemented using liquid derivatives or a notional long/short basket, it's capital-efficient: the investor's base exposure remains fully invested in equities. This design allows for the potential to enhance returns without requiring additional cash outlay.

Conceptually, it creates three complementary drivers of performance:

1. Market Return: The broad growth of U.S. equities.

2. Relative Return: The spread between strong and weak stocks.

3. Structural Efficiency: The ability to express both within one integrated framework.

Over time, the goal is to produce a return stream that compounds alongside the market but with an added layer of systematic alpha.

A Modern Expression of "Stocks for the Long Run"

Professor Siegel's enduring message has always been that equities are the asset class of long-term prosperity. By re-engineering the traditional long/short model around that principle, investors can stay rooted in the market while pursuing excess returns through disciplined, rules-based differentiation.

This is long/short reimagined for the age of factor intelligence, not a substitute for equity exposure, but a way to make the equity exposure itself work smarter.

1 Market neutral refers to a strategy with a methodology designed to minimize sensitivity to movements in the general equity market.

2 Source: J.J. Siegel, Stocks for the Long Run (6th ed.), McGraw-Hill, 2022.

3 PCA (principal component analysis) is a way to compress a huge set of features (in this case, 153 stock characteristics) into a smaller number of "hidden factors." It finds the strongest patterns or clusters of movement in the data. These hidden factors explain why many features tend to move together.

4 An autoencoder has two halves: 1) Encoder: squeezes a big set of inputs (like 153 equity features) down into a much smaller set of "latent factors." 2) Decoder: tries to rebuild the original data from those compressed factors. The only way the model learns to compress well is by identifying the most important patterns in the data.

5 The 300 long and 300 short positions is a rough guideline. Due to such factors as the assets under management in the strategy, this figure may be adjusted.

6 Shorting equities includes a cost based on borrowing the actual shares in the companies being shorted.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. The Fund invests in a basket of equity securities of large capitalization U.S. companies generally weighted by market capitalization. The Fund expects to invest most of its assets in the securities of U.S. companies and is therefore, more likely to be impacted by events or conditions affecting the United States. The Fund invests in derivatives to gain exposure to U.S. equity securities. The return on a derivative instrument may not correlate with the return of its underlying reference asset. The Fund’s use of derivatives will give rise to leverage. Derivatives can be volatile and may be less liquid than other securities. As a result, the value of an investment in the Fund may change quickly and without warning and you may lose money. While the Fund is actively managed, the Fund's investment process is heavily dependent on quantitative models and the models may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

data.title: Ryanair: Elon Musk Threatens To Buy In Starlink Spat
data.description:
Three weeks into the year, 2026 is already shaping up to be an eventful year. One item that I did not have on my checklist for 2026 was a feud between Ryanair (RYAAY) and Elon Musk, the world’s richest man. Yet, that is precisely what is happening. Musk and Ryanair are entangled in a public feud over the airline’s decision not to install Starlink on its fleet of 643 airplanes. Ryanair believes that installation and drag penalties would be too high to make it a commercial success. Musk disagrees and polled his followers on X to acquire Ryanair. In this report, I discuss the economics of installation and comment on any takeover of Ryanair by Elon Musk. I have a "Buy" rating on Ryanair, and we see that the stock has gained 14.2% since my last report while upside from FY26 earnings has not yet been priced in.

Ryanair Starlink Economics

I have modeled the Ryanair Starlink economics with fees ranging from $2 to $20. The revenue potential ranges between $400 million and $4 billion. Typically, around 6%-8% of the passengers would convert, which would yield around $24-$320 million in revenues. However, we note that Ryanair’s passengers are flying with Ryanair for its low cost. Ryanair boss O’Leary expects less than 5% to actually convert. This limits the revenue potential to $20-$200 million.

Recurring costs would be $140.5 million when we apply the numbers to Ryanair’s latest full-year results, including a $122.1 million penalty, which is less severe than the $200 million penalty that the company expects. In our assessment, I am assuming a discounted rate of $2,500 in recurring subscription fees and maintenance fees per aircraft per month. In that assessment, we note that at a price point of $10 and 8% conversion, there is a profit before installation costs. From $14 million onward, we see a more solid path for consistent profitability. If we include non-recurring costs, we get to break-even ranging between eight and 69 months, depending on the pricing and conversion rate. I believe that for investments like these, Ryanair would typically look for a return on investment larger than 100%, and that only happens from a price point higher than $16 and at an 8% conversion rate. So, for Ryanair, there does not seem to be an attractive business case, as 8% is the upper limit for conversion and unlikely to be reflective of conversion on low-cost, short-haul flights. Starlink is capable, but for Ryanair’s business, they would need high prices, and in some cases, the internet access fee would exceed Ryanair’s base fares.

Starlink believes the actual fuel burn impact would be closer to 0.3%. If we implement that, we see Starlink is already profitable when priced at $4 and a conversion rate of 5%. However, we do note that return on investment will only exceed 100% starting at $14 at a conversion rate of 6%. For a 5% conversion rate, a $16 fee would be required at least to satisfy the ROI>100% criterion. I still believe that it is unlikely that Starlink is the product that could be rolled out within Ryanair with a good return on investment. It would take a discount on the hardware and installation for Ryanair to be interesting, and perhaps that is also what Ryanair is after. They are not after increasing their brand value or reputation on a service level but are interested in keeping costs low, and that also includes costs for acquiring Starlink terminals.

Can Musk Really Buy Ryanair?

The feud between Ryanair is happening in the open and might actually be entertaining, with Musk polling his followers on whether he should buy Ryanair and replace the CEO and O’Leary dedicating an entire press conference to the economics of Starlink. While my assessment somewhat differs from the estimates of O’Leary, they do support O’Leary’s conclusion. At the same time, there are two items to note. The first one is that O'Leary tends to play things via the media to generate leverage in negotiations with suppliers. He is known for that on Boeing airplanes, as are many other airline executives who want to order Boeing or Airbus jets, and he also frequently threatens to halt routes when airport fees are not low enough. So, somewhere I do believe that Ryanair would like to have Starlink to drive ancillary revenues, but they want it at a substantially lower price point.

The second item to note is that acquiring Twitter, now X, also seemed to have started out as a joke and eventually became a reality in a $44 billion deal. Ryanair currently has a market cap of $35 billion. If you add a 30% premium, you would end up north of $45.5 billion. So, Musk could seemingly buy Ryanair for a price comparable to Twitter.

However, from a regulatory point of view, it is unlikely that this could really happen. EU ownership rules dictate that airlines must be majority owned by EU or EFTA nationals or corporations with effective EU control. So, that limits Musk to a minority stake.

In December 2020, Ryanair announced that voting restrictions pursuant to EU Regulation 1008/2008 continue to hold, meaning that non-EU owners of Ryanair shares cannot attend, speak, or vote at general meetings. That effectively means that even if Musk really wanted to buy shares, he could, but he could not vote for changes, including a change of leadership.

Conclusion: Ryanair Is Doing The Usual Ryanair Things

In some way, Ryanair is doing the usual thing. The company evaluates the appeal of an investment decision from an ROI perspective. If that is not to the company’s liking, they simply do not carry on with negotiations and instead take things to the media if necessary to generate leverage. The Starlink feud seems to be no different. It may result in Ryanair eventually getting Starlink at the price point it wants. If not, only a competitor moving in the direction of Starlink on low-cost carriers could force Ryanair’s hand to do the same thing. So, only if Ryanair is set to lose an edge to draw in cost-conscious customers or would lose a cost advantage against competitors, as was the case with Wizz Air’s upgauging move to the A321neo forcing Ryanair to eventually buy the MAX 10, would we expect Ryanair to get Starlink even at the undesired price, but not before that. I currently see no reason to change my "Buy" rating on the name.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
